title: Rabbit snuffles: pasteurellosis management
subtitle: The most common rabbit respiratory disease, how it spreads, and what can be managed.
excerpt: Snuffles is the common name for respiratory disease in rabbits caused primarily by Pasteurella multocida. It is widespread, stress-triggered, and incurable in the sense that most affected rabbits remain carriers. The practical approach is separating affected animals, reducing stressors, and working with a vet on antibiotic treatment for severe cases.
sourceNotes: Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund, health factsheets. British Veterinary Association, Pasteurella in rabbits clinical guidance. Cornell Small Farms, Rabbit Health Management bulletin.

glossaryTerms.definition: The common name for chronic or recurrent respiratory disease in rabbits, most often caused by Pasteurella multocida. Signs include nasal discharge, sneezing, eye discharge, and damp staining on the inner forepaws from the rabbit wiping its nose.

A bacterium commonly present in the nasal passages of healthy rabbits. Opportunistic: it causes disease when the rabbit is stressed, immunocompromised, or housed in poor conditions. A common cause of snuffles, inner ear disease, and abscesses in rabbits.

content.text: in their nasal passages without showing signs.
develops when stress or poor conditions allow the bacteria to proliferate. The first sign is usually a wet stain on the inner surface of the front paws, where the rabbit has been wiping its nose.

Pasteurella spreads directly between rabbits in close contact, through nasal discharge and aerosols from sneezing. In a colony system, an acutely affected rabbit will infect neighbouring animals rapidly. Stress, overcrowding, poor ventilation, and weaning are all triggers that convert a carried infection into clinical disease.

Move any rabbit showing nasal discharge, frequent sneezing, or laboured breathing into separate housing immediately. This reduces transmission and allows individual observation and treatment. Keep the separate housing clean, well-ventilated, and at a consistent temperature; cold, draughty conditions worsen respiratory disease.

A vet can prescribe antibiotic treatment for acute Pasteurella infection; the standard choices are enrofloxacin or trimethoprim-sulphonamide combinations in rabbits. Antibiotics control the infection but do not eliminate it; the rabbit remains a carrier and may relapse under stress. For a meat rabbit production system, a rabbit with recurrent snuffles is unlikely to be cost-effective to treat repeatedly; discuss the management decision with your vet.

The most effective long-term control is reducing the stressors that convert carriage to disease: avoid overcrowding, maintain ventilation without draught, minimise handling at stressful times (weaning, introduction), and source replacement breeding stock from a known clean herd. Do not introduce rabbits with active nasal discharge into a clean group.
